items.prompt: In Example 2.19, why are diagnosis codes blank for evening arrivals?
choices: The CSV file is corrupted beyond repair
The night shift used a paper fallback without a documented catch-up process
Random sampling excluded all evening patients
An API rate limit blocked night admissions
items.explain: A protocol gap at collection time produces missingness that cleaning cannot ethically invent away.

items.prompt: Which is a design-level countermeasure to channel bias at collection time?
choices: Document exclusions and add a second channel when offline populations are missing
Delete all rows with missing values without review
Ignore integration keys until the dashboard is built
items.explain: The chapter recommends documenting frames, auditing instruments, and rebalancing channels when inference matters.

items.prompt: In Example 2.22, bot filtering is part of the collection protocol before aggregates reach leaders.
items.explain: The election-week case applies bot filters during capture, before hourly aggregates are shown.

items.prompt: Why might separate traffic and air-quality vendor feeds fail to form one smart-city table?
choices: There is no shared station identifier and clock policy to join the feeds
Probability sampling is illegal for environmental data
items.explain: Integration fails at collection when identifiers and time policies are not agreed before feeds multiply.

items.prompt: Name two collection-design elements in Example 2.22 besides storing text and timestamps.
items.rubric: Bot filters, retention cap, privacy/public-post handling, API source.
items.sample_answer: The design uses an official API, applies bot filters before aggregates, caps retention to the campaign window, and follows privacy rules for public posts.
